- **Magic Lens (Optical See-Through):** You wear glasses. Through the lenses you directly see the real factory. A tiny projector inside the glasses shines a green arrow directly into your retina. The real light and the virtual light physically combine *inside your eye*.
- **Spatial AR (SAR):** No goggles at all! A ceiling projector literally **paints** a glowing path onto the factory floor. The augmentation is in the world, not in your head.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** All three analogies solve the same problem — *fusion of real and virtual* — but use radically different **light paths**. Understanding visualization techniques = understanding these light paths.

### 1.3 Why Visualization Matters in Robotics AR

In robotics, the augmentation is rarely "just a label." A robot operator may need to see:

- **Predicted trajectories** of a manipulator (requires smooth motion parallax).
- **Force/torque vectors** (requires real-world spatial alignment).
- **Occluded hazards** behind walls (requires depth-aware rendering).
- **Internal states** (battery, motor heat) overlaid on the robot body (requires surface-anchored graphics).

Hence, the choice of visualization technique directly affects **safety, situational awareness, and task accuracy**.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in AR Visualization (Bolded)

The following quantitative metrics are universally used in the AR literature and KTU-recognized standards:

- **Field of View (FoV):** typically **30°–110°** (diagonal) for consumer HMDs; **>180°** for military/aviation.
- **Resolution:** measured in **pixels per degree (PPD)**; retinal quality ≈ **60 PPD**.
- **Refresh Rate:** ≥ **60 Hz** (minimum), **90–120 Hz** preferred to avoid motion sickness.
- **Latency (Motion-to-Photon):** ≤ **20 ms** (end-to-end, KTU/Kishino benchmark).
- **Contrast Ratio:** ≥ **1000:1** for outdoor use.
- **Eye Relief:** typically **15–25 mm** for eyeglass-compatible HMDs.
- **Inter-Pupillary Distance (IPD) range:** **51–77 mm** (covers ~99% of adults).
- **Brightness:** **>2000 nits** for outdoor optical see-through.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of AR Visualization Techniques

AR visualization techniques can be classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

#### Axis A — By Display Device / Optical Path

| Sub-class | Description | Key Real-world Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Head-Mounted Display — Optical See-Through (OST-HMD)** | Real world viewed directly; virtual image optically superimposed via a beam-splitter combiner. | Microsoft HoloLens 1/2, Magic Leap 2, Vuzix M-Series |
| **Head-Mounted Display — Video See-Through (VST-HMD)** | Real world captured by stereo cameras, composited digitally, displayed on internal screens. | Meta Quest (passthrough mode), Varjo XR-3, older VR-with-cam rigs |
| **Handheld Display (Mobile AR)** | Tablet/smartphone camera + screen; the screen acts as a "magic window." | iPhone/iPad (ARKit), Android (ARCore) |
| **Spatial Augmented Reality (SAR)** | Projectors or displays embedded in the environment project directly onto real surfaces. | Automotive head-up displays (HUD), projection mapping on robots |
| **Monitor-Based (Window-on-World)** | AR rendered on a desktop monitor with a live camera feed. | AR Sandbox labs, surveillance consoles |
| **Retinal / Holographic Displays** | Lasers scan photons directly onto the retina; no intermediate screen. | Magic Leap (early prototypes), QD Laser RETISSA |
| **Contact Lens / Curved Display** | Emerging; micro-LED on a contact lens. | Mojo Vision (research), DARPA projects |

#### Axis B — By Depth/Focus Behaviour

- **Fixed-Focus (2D-over-3D):** Virtual content always in focus; cannot blur with real distance. Common in most mobile AR.
- **Multi-Focal / Varifocal:** Multiple focal planes (e.g., HoloLens 2 uses 2 planes) to mimic accommodation.
- **Light-Field / Holographic:** Reconstructs full 4D light field; correct focus at every depth.

#### Axis C — By Rendering Strategy

- **Model-Based (Geometric):** Uses 3D meshes of virtual objects, rasterized with real-camera pose.
- **Image-Based:** Uses pre-captured photographs/panoramas (e.g., Light Estimation from ARCore).
- **Hybrid / Deferred:** Combines screen-space techniques (e.g., screen-space reflections) with virtual geometry.

### 2.2 Operating Principles of the Three Dominant Techniques

#### 2.2.1 Optical See-Through HMD (OST-HMD) — Detailed Principle

The OST-HMD uses a **partially reflective beam-splitter** (a "combiner") placed in front of the eye. Real-world light from the environment passes through the combiner mostly unattenuated. Simultaneously, light from a small internal micro-display (LCoS, OLED, micro-LED, or laser) is reflected off the combiner *into* the eye.

**Architecture flow:**

$$ \text{Real World} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Transmit}}\; \text{Combiner} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Superpose}}\; \text{Eye (Retina)} $$

$$ \text{Micro-display} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Reflect off combiner}}\; \text{Eye (Retina)} $$

**Optical path condition** — the virtual image distance $D_v$ is set by the lens equation:

$$ \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{D_o} + \frac{1}{D_v} $$

where $f$ is the focal length of the combiner/eyepiece lens, $D_o$ is the object distance to the micro-display, and $D_v$ is the perceived distance of the virtual image.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why OST is hard for robotics:** Because the eye receives real light *unmediated*, any misalignment between real and virtual cannot be "digitally corrected" — the error is physical. Registration tolerances are therefore < 1 mrad for surgical/robotic applications.

#### 2.2.2 Video See-Through HMD (VST-HMD) — Detailed Principle

VST replaces the optical combiner with a **digital compositor**. Two (or more) outward-facing cameras capture the world; the GPU composites a virtual layer on top of the camera video using the camera's known intrinsics, extrinsics, and a synchronized pose; finally the result is shown on internal LCD/OLED panels placed right in front of the eyes.

**Architecture flow:**

$$ \text{Real World} \;\to\; \text{Stereo Camera} \;\to\; \text{GPU Compositor} \;\to\; \text{Internal Display} \;\to\; \text{Eye} $$

**Digital registration model:**

$$ \mathbf{p}_{\text{out}} = K \left( R \;\vert\; t \right) \mathbf{X} $$

where $\mathbf{p}_{\text{out}}$ is the homogeneous 2D pixel, $K$ is the intrinsic matrix of the display, and $(R\mid t)$ is the user-pose.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why VST is preferred for robotics:** Full control of the image allows **occlusion rendering**, **real-time relighting**, and **faked focus blur**. The trade-off is added latency (camera → GPU → display) and reduced peripheral resolution.

#### 2.2.3 Spatial Augmented Reality (SAR)

SAR removes the head-mounted device entirely. The "augmenter" is the **environment itself**. A calibrated projector (or array of projectors) emits graphics directly onto the physical robot/workspace, where the human observes the projection naturally.

**Pixel-to-surface projection model:**

$$ \mathbf{x}_{\text{world}} = \Pi^{-1}\bigl(\mathbf{x}_{\text{proj}}\bigr) $$

where $\Pi$ is the projector's calibrated mapping, and $\mathbf{x}_{\text{world}}$ is the 3D point the pixel illuminates after intersecting the (known) surface geometry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why SAR suits industrial robotics:** Multiple operators can see the augmentation **simultaneously without eyewear**; ideal for factory floors. Drawback: surfaces must be known and non-occluding.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Thin-lens imaging (combiner optics) | $\dfrac{1}{f}=\dfrac{1}{D_o}+\dfrac{1}{D_v}$ | $f$ = focal length (m), $D_o$ = object distance, $D_v$ = virtual-image distance |
| 2 | Pinhole camera projection | $\mathbf{p}=K\!\left[R\mid t\right]\mathbf{X}$ | $K$ = $3\times3$ intrinsics, $R,t$ = extrinsics, $\mathbf{X}$ = 3D world point |
| 3 | Field of View (horizontal) | $\text{FoV}_h = 2\arctan\!\dfrac{w}{2f}$ | $w$ = sensor width, $f$ = focal length |
| 4 | Resolution (PPD) | $\text{PPD} = \dfrac{\text{horizontal pixels}}{\text{FoV}_h}$ | unitless, target $\geq 60$ |
| 5 | Vergence–Accommodation conflict | $\Delta_{\text{VAC}} = \vert D_{\text{vergence}} - D_{\text{accommodation}}\vert$ | distance, ideally $\leq 0.3$ D |
| 6 | Motion-to-photon latency budget | $L_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{track}} + L_{\text{render}} + L_{\text{display}}$ | $L_{\text{total}} \leq 20$ ms |
| 7 | Eye relief for combiner | $d_{\text{ER}} \geq 15\text{ mm}$ (eyeglass compatible) | $d_{\text{ER}}$ = distance from eye to combiner |
| 8 | Brightness for outdoor OST | $L_{\text{display}} \geq 2000 \text{ nits}$ | $L$ = luminance |
| 9 | Projector pixel-to-world mapping | $\mathbf{x}_{\text{world}} = \Pi^{-1}(\mathbf{x}_{\text{proj}})$ | $\Pi$ = calibrated projector function |
| 10 | Stereo disparity (depth from stereo) | $Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{d}$ | $B$ = baseline, $d$ = disparity (pixels) |
| 11 | Refresh rate threshold (sickness) | $f_{\text{refresh}} \geq 60\text{ Hz}$ (preferably 90–120) | $f$ in Hz |
| 12 | Effective transmittance of combiner | $T_{\text{eff}} \cdot R_{\text{eff}} \leq 1$ | $T$ transmittance, $R$ reflectance, $T+R\leq 1$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Visualization Used | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Industrial Robotics (KUKA, FANUC)** | VST-HMD + projection SAR | Trajectory preview, safety zones, AR-guided teach pendant |
| **Surgical Robotics (Da Vinci)** | OST-HMD with high PPD | Overlay preoperative scans onto patient anatomy |
| **Autonomous Vehicle R&D** | HUD (SAR) | Display speed, navigation, hazard cues on windshield |
| **Warehouse Logistics** | Mobile AR (ARKit/ARCore) | Pick-by-vision: arrows on shelves |
| **Military / Aviation** | OST-HMD (F-35 Gen III HMDS) | Cueing, FLIR fusion |
| **Field Maintenance (aerospace, power)** | OST + VST hybrid | Annotate parts with torque values, exploded views |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Robotics applications almost always demand a **hybrid** approach — VST for digital perfection during planning, OST for low-latency execution, and SAR for shared situational awareness among multiple operators.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Field of View from a Pinhole Camera Model

A combiner lens or camera forms a **cone of light** bounded by the sensor/display and the focal length. Deriving the horizontal FoV:

**Step 1:** Consider a symmetric lens with focal length $f$ and a rectangular sensor of horizontal width $w$ centered on the optical axis.

**Step 2:** The half-angle $\theta_h$ subtended at the lens by the right edge of the sensor is given by basic trigonometry:

$$ \tan \theta_h = \dfrac{(w/2)}{f} $$

**Step 3:** The full horizontal field of view is twice this half-angle:

$$ \text{FoV}_h = 2 \theta_h = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{w}{2f}\right) $$

**Step 4:** For an HMD using a micro-display of width $w$ and an eyepiece of focal length $f$, the *user-perceived* FoV is:

$$ \text{FoV}_h = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{w}{2f}\right) $$

**Step 5:** Numerical example. HoloLens 2 uses $w \approx 12.6$ mm and $f \approx 13.1$ mm:

$$ \text{FoV}_h = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{6.3}{13.1}\right) = 2 \arctan(0.4809) = 2(25.69^\circ) \approx 51.4^\circ $$

This matches the published 52° diagonal figure (rounded).

### 3.2 Derivation — Motion-to-Photon Latency Budget

The total latency between an operator's head motion and the augmented image reaching the retina is the **sum of three sequential delays**:

**Step 1:** Tracking delay $L_{\text{track}}$ — IMU + camera fusion, typically 1–3 ms.

**Step 2:** Rendering delay $L_{\text{render}}$ — pose update, draw call, scan-out, typically 8–15 ms.

**Step 3:** Display persistence delay $L_{\text{display}}$ — pixel response + persistence, typically 3–7 ms.

$$ L_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{track}} + L_{\text{render}} + L_{\text{display}} $$

**Step 4:** Substitute typical values: $L_{\text{total}} = 2 + 11 + 5 = 18$ ms.

**Step 5:** Convert to human perception. At a 60 Hz refresh, one frame = 16.67 ms. The KTU-cited threshold for comfortable AR is $L_{\text{total}} \leq 20$ ms; medical-grade AR aims for $\leq 5$ ms.

### 3.3 Derivation — Vergence–Accommodation Conflict Metric

The human visual system has two coupled depth cues:

- **Vergence:** angle between the two eyes' lines of sight, $\theta_v = 2\arctan(B/2Z_v)$, where $B$ is IPD and $Z_v$ is vergence depth.
- **Accommodation:** crystalline lens curvature, focusing at $Z_a$.

In AR, a flat display always forces $Z_a = d_{\text{display}}$ (e.g., 2 m in HoloLens 2) regardless of $Z_v$. The conflict is:

$$ \Delta_{\text{VAC}} = \left\vert D_{\text{vergence}} - D_{\text{accommodation}} \right\vert $$

where $D = 1/Z$ is the dioptric value (m⁻¹). Ideally $\Delta_{\text{VAC}} \leq 0.3$ D for comfortable long-duration viewing.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing a Visualization Technique

The following **decision matrix** is essential for KTU design problems:

| Criterion | OST-HMD | VST-HMD | SAR (Projector) | Mobile (Handheld) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Real-world brightness preserved | ★★★★★ | ★★ (camera-limited) | ★★★★★ | ★★ (camera-limited) |
| Latency achievable | ★★★★ (5–11 ms) | ★★★ (15–25 ms) | ★★★★★ (no head pose) | ★★★ |
| Occlusion of real by virtual | ★ (poor) | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ (projection shadow) | ★★★★ |
| Multiple viewers | ★ | ★ | ★★★★★ | ★★ |
| Mobility / untethered | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ (projector setup) | ★★★★★ |
| Cost | ★★ | ★★ | ★★★ (calibration) | ★★★★★ |
| Suitability for **robotics teleop** | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU tip:** When asked "which technique is best for a given scenario," read each row of this matrix against the application requirement and justify with a numeric metric from Section 2.3.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Stereo Depth for VST Robotic AR

A VST-HMD has two cameras with focal length $f = 800$ pixels and baseline $B = 64$ mm. The robot's gripper is detected with disparity $d = 24$ pixels. Compute depth $Z$.

**Step 1:** Use the standard stereo depth formula:

$$ Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{d} $$

**Step 2:** Substitute, keeping units consistent. Convert $B$ to match $f$ units: $B = 64$ mm, but with $f$ in pixels we need $B$ in the same world unit that $d$ will be expressed in. The disparity $d$ is in pixels, so we use:

$$ Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{d} = \dfrac{800 \times 64\text{ mm}}{24} = \dfrac{51\,200}{24}\text{ mm} $$

**Step 3:** Evaluate:

$$ Z = 2133.33 \text{ mm} \approx 2.13 \text{ m} $$

**Step 4:** Verify depth uncertainty. A 1-pixel disparity error gives:

$$ \delta Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{d(d-1)} - Z = \dfrac{51\,200}{24 \times 23} - 2133.33 \approx 92.75 - 2.13 \approx 90.6 \text{ mm} $$

This shows that **small disparities (far objects) lead to large depth errors** — a key design constraint for AR visualization of distant robots.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — AR Visualization Taxonomy (Module-Level)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[AR Visualization Techniques]
    A --> B[Head-Mounted Displays]
    A --> C[Spatial AR]
    A --> D[Handheld Mobile AR]
    A --> E[Monitor Based AR]
    A --> F[Retinal Holographic]

    B --> B1[Optical See Through HMD]
    B --> B2[Video See Through HMD]
    B1 --> B1a[Beam Splitter Combiner]
    B1 --> B1b[Micro Display OLED or LCoS]
    B1 --> B1c[Hololens 2 Magic Leap 2]
    B2 --> B2a[Stereo Camera Capture]
    B2 --> B2b[GPU Compositor]
    B2 --> B2c[Varjo XR 3 Quest Passthrough]

    C --> C1[Projector Mapping]
    C --> C2[Head Up Display HUD]
    C --> C3[Digital Light Processing DLP]

    D --> D1[Smartphone ARKit ARCore]
    D --> D2[Tablet AR]

    E --> E1[Webcam AR Sandbox]
    E --> E2[Surveillance Console]

    F --> F1[Laser Retinal Scan]
    F --> F2[Light Field Display]

    B1c --> R1[Robotics Trajectory Overlay]
    B2c --> R2[Robotic Teleop with Relighting]
    C1 --> R3[Factory Floor Safety Zones]
    D1 --> R4[Pick by Vision Warehouse]
    C2 --> R5[Vehicle Nav AR HUD]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Flow of a VST-HMD Visualization Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Real World Scene] --> B[Stereo Camera Pair]
    B --> C[Image Signal Processor ISP]
    C --> D[SLAM and Pose Estimation]
    D --> E[Virtual Scene Graph]
    E --> F[GPU Compositor]
    C --> F
    F --> G[Display Panel Left]
    F --> H[Display Panel Right]
    G --> I[User Left Eye]
    H --> J[User Right Eye]
    I --> K[Perceived AR Image]
    J --> K
    D --> L[Robot Telemetry Stream]
    L --> E
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Light-Path of OST-HMD vs VST-HMD vs SAR

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph OST[OST HMD Light Path]
        A1[Real World] -->|direct transmit| C1[Combiner]
        B1[Micro Display] -->|reflect| C1
        C1 --> E1[Eye]
    end

    subgraph VST[VST HMD Light Path]
        A2[Real World] --> Cam[Camera Sensor]
        Cam --> GPU[GPU Compositor]
        GPU --> D2[Internal Display]
        D2 --> E2[Eye]
    end

    subgraph SAR[SAR Light Path]
        A3[Projector] -->|emits light| S3[Real Surface]
        S3 -->|reflected to| E3[Eye]
    end
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block — Render Pipeline for AR Visualization (Robot Telemetry)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Telemetry Packet] --> Parse[Parse Joint States]
    Parse --> FK[Forward Kinematics]
    FK --> Mesh[Generate 3D Overlay Mesh]
    Pose[Head Pose from SLAM] --> Cam[Virtual Camera]
    Mesh --> Cam
    Cam --> Raster[Rasterize with Real Lights]
    Raster --> Compose[Composite with Real Frame]
    Compose --> Out[Final AR Image to Display]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Usage Tip for KTU:** In the 14-mark design questions, you are expected to draw a *functional block diagram* of the visualization pipeline. These four Mermaid blocks cover the four most likely answer patterns: taxonomy, VST pipeline, light-path comparison, and rendering flow.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (PERUT755)]**
Differentiate between **Optical See-Through HMD** and **Video See-Through HMD** in terms of the way the real-world image is delivered to the user's eye.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **OST-HMD:** The real-world image reaches the eye *directly* through a partially reflective optical combiner. The eye sees the real scene unmediated; the virtual image is *added* via reflection off the combiner. (1 mark)
- **VST-HMD:** The real-world image is first captured by one or more cameras, then digitized, composited with virtual content by a GPU, and finally shown on a near-eye display panel placed in front of the eye. The user *never* sees the real world directly. (1 mark)
- **Key consequence:** OST preserves real-world brightness and resolution perfectly but cannot digitally occlude real objects; VST allows full digital control (occlusion, relighting) at the cost of latency, resolution, and color fidelity. (1 mark)

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (PERUT755)]**
Define **Spatial Augmented Reality (SAR)** and state **two** advantages it has over head-mounted AR for a multi-operator robotic work cell.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Spatial Augmented Reality is a visualization technique in which the virtual content is *projected directly onto the real environment* (typically using calibrated projectors) or displayed via in-environment screens, rather than being shown on a personal head-mounted or handheld display. The augmentation exists *in the world*, decoupled from any single user.

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**
1. **Multiple simultaneous users** can perceive the same augmentation without donning any device — useful when a robot cell is supervised by several engineers.
2. **Zero head-mounted latency** — the augmentation is anchored in space, not to a moving user's eyes, so the motion-to-photon problem is eliminated for the display itself.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PERUT755]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the **operating principle of an Optical See-Through HMD**, with a neat block diagram showing the optical path of real and virtual light. Discuss the trade-offs in **field of view vs. eye-box size**.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the **motion-to-photon latency budget** for an AR-HMD visualization pipeline and explain why a value **> 20 ms** degrades the operator's situational awareness in a robotic teleoperation task.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Operating Principle of OST-HMD [7 marks]:**

An Optical See-Through Head-Mounted Display superimposes computer-generated imagery onto the user's direct view of the real world using a **partially reflective optical combiner** placed in front of the eye.

**Block diagram (describe in text — 2 marks):**

```
Real World ──► (transmit) ──► Combiner ──► Eye
                                     ▲
Micro-display ──► Eyepiece ──► (reflect)┘
```

**Working steps (3 marks):**
1. A small **micro-display** (LCoS, OLED, micro-LED, or laser) renders the virtual scene.
2. An **eyepiece/collimating lens** projects the micro-display image to optical infinity or to a chosen virtual distance $D_v$.
3. The **partially silvered beam-splitter (combiner)** reflects a fraction of the virtual light into the eye, while transmitting most of the real-world light from the environment.
4. The retina thus receives a **superposition** of real and virtual photons, perceived as a single fused image.

**Trade-off: FoV vs. Eye-box [2 marks]:**
- FoV is governed by $\text{FoV} = 2\arctan(w/2f)$ where $w$ is the micro-display width and $f$ the eyepiece focal length. Increasing $w$ or decreasing $f$ widens FoV.
- A larger eye-box (the volume in which the pupil can move while still seeing the image) requires a larger exit pupil, which in turn demands a brighter micro-display.
- A wider FoV demands a *larger combiner surface*, which makes the device bulky and the eye-box fragile — HoloLens 2 deliberately chose a ~52° FoV to balance this.

**(b) Motion-to-Photon Latency Derivation [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 [1 mark]:** Define motion-to-photon latency $L_{\text{total}}$ as the time elapsed between the user's head motion and the corresponding updated pixel reaching the retina.

**Step 2 [2 marks]:** Decompose the pipeline into three sequential stages:

$$ L_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{track}} + L_{\text{render}} + L_{\text{display}} $$

- $L_{\text{track}}$ — IMU + camera-based pose estimation (1–3 ms).
- $L_{\text{render}}$ — pose application, scene graph update, GPU rasterization, scan-out (8–15 ms).
- $L_{\text{display}}$ — pixel persistence, hold time (3–7 ms).

**Step 3 [2 marks]:** Numerical example. With $L_{\text{track}} = 2$ ms, $L_{\text{render}} = 11$ ms, $L_{\text{display}} = 5$ ms:

$$ L_{\text{total}} = 2 + 11 + 5 = 18 \text{ ms} \leq 20 \text{ ms} \;\;\text{(within budget)} $$

**Step 4 [2 marks]:** Why > 20 ms hurts teleop:
- The user moves their head; the AR overlay lags behind, producing a **perceived offset** between robot and its virtual annotation.
- At head angular velocity $\omega \approx 50°/\text{s}$ (typical turn), an extra 20 ms creates a **1° angular error** — equivalent to ~36 mm positional error at 2 m working distance.
- The mismatch triggers **simulator sickness** symptoms (nausea, disorientation) and degrades grasping accuracy, which is unacceptable in robotic teleop.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** A common mistake is forgetting the *display persistence* term. Many students only count rendering latency and miss the ~5 ms pixel-hold contribution, which is precisely what fails the 20 ms budget in poorly designed systems.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PERUT755]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a comparative table, discuss the **relative merits of Optical See-Through, Video See-Through, and Spatial Augmented Reality** for visualizing **robot trajectories** in an industrial work cell.

**(b) [7 marks]** A VST-HMD for a robotic teleop system has two cameras with focal length $f = 1000$ pixels and baseline $B = 70$ mm. The robot gripper is observed with a disparity of $d = 35$ pixels. Calculate (i) the depth of the gripper from the cameras, and (ii) the depth error if the disparity estimation has a 1-pixel uncertainty.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparative Table & Analysis [7 marks]:**

| Aspect | OST-HMD | VST-HMD | SAR (Projector) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Real-world fidelity** | Highest (direct view) | Limited by camera (dynamic range, latency) | High (direct view of unaltered real world) |
| **Virtual-real alignment tolerance** | Sub-mrad (physical) | Sub-pixel (digital) | Sub-pixel on calibrated surface |
| **Multi-user support** | No (personal) | No (personal) | **Yes (inherently shared)** |
| **Latency budget** | 5–15 ms | 15–30 ms | N/A (head pose irrelevant) |
| **Occlusion handling** | None (virtual cannot hide real) | Full (real-z-buffer) | Partial (via projector shadow geometry) |
| **Setup / calibration** | Per-user IPD | Per-user + per-camera | Complex projector–surface calibration |
| **Best for trajectories** | Personal trajectory preview | Precise occlusion-aware preview | Shared safety-zone visualization |

**Synthesis (1 mark for justification):** In a *multi-operator* industrial cell, **SAR** is best for showing **shared safety fences**; for an *individual* operator doing *precise grasping*, **VST** is preferred because it allows the digital overlay to correctly **occlude the gripper** as it enters a fixture; **OST** is best for *low-latency peripersonal* guidance where the operator's view of the real robot must remain undistorted.

**(b) Stereo Depth Calculation [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 [1 mark]:** State the standard stereo depth formula:

$$ Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{d} $$

**Step 2 [2 marks]:** Substitute the given values, ensuring unit consistency (focal length in pixels, baseline in mm, disparity in pixels, depth in mm):

$$ Z = \dfrac{1000 \times 70}{35} = \dfrac{70\,000}{35} = 2000 \text{ mm} = 2.0 \text{ m} $$

**Step 3 [1 mark]:** State the result: **The gripper is 2.0 m from the camera pair.**

**Step 4 [2 marks]:** Depth-error derivation for 1-pixel disparity uncertainty. Let $d' = d \pm 1$:

$$ Z_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{d - 1} = \dfrac{70\,000}{34} \approx 2058.82 \text{ mm} $$

$$ \delta Z = Z_{\text{new}} - Z = 2058.82 - 2000.00 = 58.82 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 5 [1 mark]:** Interpret: a 1-pixel disparity error causes ~59 mm of depth uncertainty at 2 m. This must be communicated to the operator via the AR overlay's confidence indicator — visualizing a **bounding-box** rather than a precise point.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Two very common mistakes in this problem:
> 1. Mixing units — using the baseline in meters without converting (gives Z = 2/35 m, which is wrong).
> 2. Forgetting to express the depth *and* the error in **consistent units** (the answer should be in mm or m, not a mix).
> 3. Failing to interpret the depth error in the **robotics context** — i.e., explaining that this error directly limits safe approach velocity of the gripper.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Visualization** is the *delivery* of augmented content to the eye; it is downstream of tracking/registration.
- ✅ Three dominant techniques: **OST-HMD** (real light transmitted, virtual reflected), **VST-HMD** (real captured, digital compositing, display), **SAR** (project into world).
- ✅ OST preserves real-world brightness perfectly; VST allows digital occlusion; SAR supports multiple viewers.
- ✅ **Combiner** in OST is a partially reflective surface; obeys $T + R \leq 1$.
- ✅ **Thin-lens equation** $\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{D_o} + \frac{1}{D_v}$ governs virtual-image distance in OST.
- ✅ **Field of view** $\text{FoV}_h = 2\arctan(w/2f)$ — wider FoV requires larger combiner and brighter source.
- ✅ **Motion-to-photon latency** $L_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{track}} + L_{\text{render}} + L_{\text{display}} \leq 20$ ms.
- ✅ **Vergence–Accommodation Conflict** is the mismatch between $D_{\text{vergence}}$ and $D_{\text{accommodation}}$ in flat-panel HMDs; < 0.3 D is the comfort threshold.
- ✅ **Stereo depth** $Z = fB/d$ — small disparity ⇒ large depth uncertainty.
- ✅ **Mobile AR** (ARKit/ARCore) is *VST* on a phone — the *screen* is the display and the *back camera* is the real-world sensor.
- ✅ **Spatial AR** needs **calibrated projector–surface mapping** $\mathbf{x}_{\text{world}} = \Pi^{-1}(\mathbf{x}_{\text{proj}})$.
- ✅ For **robotics teleoperation**, VST-HMD is generally preferred for *individual precise tasks*; SAR is preferred for *shared situational awareness*; OST is preferred for *low-latency peripersonal guidance*.
- ✅ **PPD ≥ 60** for retinal quality; **refresh ≥ 90 Hz** preferred; **brightness ≥ 2000 nits** for outdoor OST.
- ✅ **Eye relief ≥ 15 mm** for eyeglass compatibility; **IPD range 51–77 mm** covers ~99% of adults.
- ✅ Be ready to draw: (i) a comparative light-path diagram, (ii) a VST rendering pipeline, (iii) a stereo depth derivation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AR for Human Robot Communication and Cobots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT755 - VISUAL AND AUGMENTED REALITY FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** AR for Human Robot Communication and Cobots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**AR for Human-Robot Communication (HRC)** refers to the use of Augmented Reality interfaces — spatially registered graphical, textual, or symbolic overlays — to convey robot state, intent, perception, and planned actions to a human operator or collaborator within the robot's operating workspace.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cobot (Collaborative Robot)** — Per **ISO/TS 15066**, a cobot is a robot designed for direct interaction with a human within a **collaborative workspace**, where the robot and human can perform tasks simultaneously while sharing the same operational volume.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for PERUT755, AR-based HRC is positioned as the *transparency layer* that converts a robot's opaque internal decision processes (planner outputs, belief states, probabilistic maps) into **intuitive, spatial, visual cues** that align with how humans naturally interpret the physical world.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Robot's Thought Bubble"**
> Imagine a friend wearing a VR headset who is about to hand you a glass of water but cannot see you clearly. They might say, *"I'm reaching forward at chest height in 2 seconds."* Now imagine instead that their **intended hand trajectory appears as a glowing arrow in mid-air**, projected from their shoulder onto the cup. That glowing arrow is **Augmented Reality for Human-Robot Communication**. The robot is no longer a black box — you can *see what it is about to do, where it believes objects are, and which zones it considers safe.*

### 1.3 Physical / Standardized Metrics in AR-Cobot Systems

| Metric | Standard Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Maximum cobot payload** | **≤ 35 kg** (ISO/TS 15066) | Limits kinetic energy in collision |
| **Max quasi-static contact pressure** | **≤ 140 N/cm²** (hand region) | Biomechanical injury threshold |
| **Max transient contact force** | **≤ 280 N** | Pain threshold limit |
| **Maximum collaborative speed** | **≤ 250 mm/s** | Default safe approach speed |
| **AR overlay registration latency** | **≤ 20 ms** | Perceptual smoothness threshold |
| **Human-machine separation distance** | **≥ 0.5 m** | ISO 10218-1 default |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of AR-Based Human-Robot Communication

**Pillar 1 — Intent Visualization**
The robot projects its future trajectory, target object, or action plan onto the shared workspace using AR cues:
- **Trajectory arrows** (curved Bézier paths)
- **Goal markers** (spheres, cubes, or halos)
- **Action labels** ("PICK", "PLACE", "WAIT")

**Pillar 2 — State & Perception Visualization**
The robot's belief about the world is overlaid as AR content:
- Occupancy grid maps rendered as semi-transparent point clouds
- Detected humans shown as skeletons (pose estimation)
- Object bounding boxes with class labels and confidence scores
- Force/torque readings as numeric HUDs

**Pillar 3 — Safety & Trust Calibration**
AR provides *legible safety boundaries*:
- **Red zones**: Robot's reach envelope
- **Yellow zones**: Caution / reduced speed
- **Green zones**: Verified safe to enter
- **Stop buttons**: Floating 3D affordances for emergency halt

### 2.2 Why AR Outperforms Conventional 2D Interfaces

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Conventional HMIs (screens, tablets) require the human to **mentally rotate** 2D data into 3D space — a process that consumes cognitive load and introduces interpretation errors. AR delivers information **already pre-registered to the physical scene**, reducing the *transformational distance* between perception and action.

### 2.3 Collaborative Robot Operating Modes (ISO/TS 15066)

There are **four canonical cobot operating modes** that AR interfaces must support:

1. **Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS)** — Robot stops when human enters workspace.
2. **Hand Guiding** — Operator physically moves the end-effector; AR shows recorded path.
3. **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)** — Robot speed adapts to human proximity; AR visualizes the separation distance.
4. **Power and Force Limiting (PFL)** — Robot's mechanical output is biomechanically capped; AR confirms compliance.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` for absolute value in tables to avoid breaking markdown syntax.

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Minimum separation distance (SSM)** | $S_{min} \geq v_{robot} \cdot T_{reaction} + S_{human} + S_{margin}$ | meters (m) |
| 2 | **Stopping distance** | $S_{stop} = v \cdot t_{r} + \dfrac{v^{2}}{2 a_{max}}$ | meters (m) |
| 3 | **Kinetic energy of cobot** | $E_{k} = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ | Joules (J) |
| 4 | **Quasi-static contact pressure** | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | N/m² or N/cm² |
| 5 | **AR registration error budget** | $E_{total} = \sqrt{E_{track}^{2} + E_{calib}^{2} + E_{drift}^{2}}$ | pixels or mm |
| 6 | **Latency threshold for comfort** | $L_{max} \leq \dfrac{1}{4} \cdot \dfrac{1}{f_{display}}$ | seconds |
| 7 | **AR pointing accuracy (Fitts' Law)** | $MT = a + b \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | milliseconds |
| 8 | **Confidence score (perception)** | $C = \dfrac{P(\text{correct})}{P(\text{correct}) + P(\text{incorrect})}$ | dimensionless [0,1] |
| 9 | **Cognitive load reduction (relative)** | $\Delta_{load} = \dfrac{CL_{baseline} - CL_{AR}}{CL_{baseline}} \cdot 100\%$ | percent (%) |
| 10 | **Trust calibration ratio** | $T_{ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Perceived reliability}}{\text{Actual reliability}}$ | dimensionless |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing**: AR cobot programming eliminates 80% of teach-pendant training time.
- **Surgical Robotics**: AR overlays pre-operative plans on patient anatomy, enabling safer human-surgeon-robot collaboration.
- **Disaster Response**: AR telepresence lets remote operators understand a robot's spatial reasoning.
- **Rehabilitation**: AR-cobot systems assist stroke patients by visualizing intended motion support.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Minimum Separation Distance for SSM Mode

The **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)** operating mode is the most AR-relevant cobot mode because the *separation distance* must be visualized in real-time as a dynamic AR zone.

**Step 1 — State the physical constraint.**

In SSM mode, the protective stop distance must satisfy:

$$
S_{protective} \leq S_{min}(t)
$$

This means the robot must *always* be able to come to a complete stop *before* contacting the human.

**Step 2 — Decompose $S_{min}(t)$ into constituent distances.**

The minimum separation is the sum of:
- The distance the **human** can move toward the robot during the reaction time
- The distance the **robot** moves during its own reaction time
- The **stopping distance** of the robot after brakes engage
- A **safety margin** to absorb measurement noise

$$
S_{min} = S_{human} + S_{robot\_reaction} + S_{stopping} + S_{margin}
$$

**Step 3 — Express each term mathematically.**

The human's encroachment in reaction time $T_{r}$:

$$
S_{human} = v_{human} \cdot T_{r}
$$

The robot's movement during its own sensing + processing delay $t_{r}$:

$$
S_{robot\_reaction} = v_{robot} \cdot t_{r}
$$

**Step 4 — Derive the stopping distance using kinematics.**

After brakes apply, with maximum deceleration $a_{max}$, the robot decelerates from velocity $v$ to $0$:

$$
v_{final}^{2} = v_{initial}^{2} - 2 a_{max} \cdot S_{stopping}
$$

Setting $v_{final} = 0$:

$$
0 = v^{2} - 2 a_{max} \cdot S_{stopping}
$$

Solving for $S_{stopping}$:

$$
S_{stopping} = \dfrac{v^{2}}{2 a_{max}}
$$

**Step 5 — Combine all terms into the master formula.**

$$
\boxed{\;S_{min} = v_{human} T_{r} + v_{robot} t_{r} + \dfrac{v_{robot}^{2}}{2 a_{max}} + S_{margin}\;}
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation.**

- As $v_{robot}$ increases, $S_{min}$ grows **quadratically** — this is why cobots are speed-limited.
- The AR overlay must update $S_{min}$ in real-time and color-code it (e.g., red = imminent stop required, green = safe).
- For a UR5e cobot ($a_{max} \approx 1.5$ m/s²) at $v_{robot} = 0.25$ m/s, $T_{r} = t_{r} = 0.1$ s, $v_{human} = 1.6$ m/s, $S_{margin} = 0.05$ m:

$$
S_{min} = (1.6)(0.1) + (0.25)(0.1) + \dfrac{(0.25)^{2}}{2(1.5)} + 0.05
$$

$$
S_{min} = 0.160 + 0.025 + 0.0208 + 0.050 = 0.2558 \text{ m}
$$

So the AR visualization must enforce a $\sim 0.26$ m buffer between human skeleton and robot envelope.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — AR Registration Error Budget

For AR overlays to be *trustworthy* in HRC, registration error must be sub-centimeter. The total error is composed of three independent noise sources.

**Step 1 — Define sources of error.**
- $E_{track}$: SLAM/inside-out tracking noise (subpixel-marker or feature-based)
- $E_{calib}$: Camera-IMU extrinsics + display calibration error
- $E_{drift}$: Time-dependent pose drift over a session

**Step 2 — Treat as orthogonal Gaussian noise (RSS model).**

$$
E_{total} = \sqrt{E_{track}^{2} + E_{calib}^{2} + E_{drift}^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Insert representative values.**
- $E_{track} = 2$ mm (modern inside-out tracker)
- $E_{calib} = 1.5$ mm
- $E_{drift} = 3$ mm (over 30 minutes)

$$
E_{total} = \sqrt{2^{2} + 1.5^{2} + 3^{2}} = \sqrt{4 + 2.25 + 9} = \sqrt{15.25} \approx 3.9 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
A $\sim 4$ mm error is acceptable for trajectory visualization but **insufficient for surgical cobot applications**, where AR-assisted robotic surgery demands $E_{total} \leq 1$ mm, requiring additional fiducials and depth sensors.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — AR Cue Mapping for Cobot Modes

| Cobot Mode | AR Cue Type | Color Encoding | Update Rate | Trust Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SRMS** | Robot envelope halo | Red (active) / Gray (idle) | 10 Hz | Confirms stop state |
| **Hand Guiding** | Recorded path trail | Blue gradient (time) | 30 Hz | Validates programming |
| **SSM** | Distance ring + numeric | Green → Yellow → Red | 60 Hz | Calibrates speed expectation |
| **PFL** | Force-torque HUD | Yellow bars | 100 Hz | Confirms safety compliance |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Safeguards Applied
- All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`) — no reserved keywords used.
- Node labels contain **plain uppercase alphanumeric text only** (no markdown formatting tags).

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — AR-Cobot Communication Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[ROBOT PLANNER CORE] --> node2[TASK INTENT BUFFER]
    node2 --> node3[PATH TRAJECTORY GENERATOR]
    node3 --> node4[AR SCENE GRAPH BUILDER]
    node4 --> node5[SPATIAL REGISTRATION MODULE]
    node5 --> node6[AR HEADSET OR MOBILE DEVICE]
    node6 --> node7[HUMAN OPERATOR PERCEPTION]
    node7 --> node8[VOICE OR GESTURE INPUT]
    node8 --> node9[INTENT RECOGNITION MODULE]
    node9 --> node2
    node1 --> node10[SAFETY SUPERVISOR ISO TS 15066]
    node10 --> node11[COLLABORATIVE ZONE MANAGER]
    node11 --> node6
    node11 --> node12[PROTECTIVE STOP TRIGGER]
    node7 --> node11
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Flow — AR Visualization Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sensorA[RGB CAMERA] --> fusionA[SENSOR FUSION NODE]
    sensorB[DEPTH SENSOR] --> fusionA
    sensorC[IMU UNIT] --> fusionA
    fusionA --> slamA[VISUAL INERTIAL SLAM]
    slamA --> poseA[6DOF CAMERA POSE]
    poseA --> calibA[ROBOT TO CAMERA CALIBRATION]
    calibA --> overlayA[AR OVERLAY RENDERER]
    robotA[ROBOT CONTROLLER] --> stateA[ROBOT STATE POLLER]
    stateA --> overlayA
    overlayA --> outputA[STEREO DISPLAY OUTPUT]
    outputA --> humanA[HUMAN COLLABORATOR]
    humanA --> feedbackA[FEEDBACK LOOP]
    feedbackA --> robotA
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree — AR Safety Zone Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    detA[HUMAN POSE DETECTED] --> distA{COMPUTE DISTANCE D}
    distA --> brA{D GREATER THAN 1.5 METERS}
    brA --> greenA[GREEN ZONE NORMAL SPEED]
    brA --> brB{D BETWEEN 0.5 AND 1.5 METERS}
    brB --> yellowA[YELLOW ZONE SPEED REDUCED]
    brB --> brC{D LESS THAN 0.5 METERS}
    brC --> redA[RED ZONE PROTECTIVE STOP]
    redA --> haltA[ACTIVATE ROBOT BRAKES]
    yellowA --> monitorA[CONTINUOUS MONITORING AT 60 HZ]
    greenA --> monitorA
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Model Question, PERUT755]`
*Define a Cobot as per ISO/TS 15066. List the four collaborative operating modes.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Collaborative Robot (Cobot)** is a robot designed for direct operation within a workspace shared with a human, conforming to the safety guidelines of **ISO/TS 15066**.
The four collaborative operating modes are:
1. **Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS)** — Robot halts when human enters workspace.
2. **Hand Guiding** — Operator manually moves the end-effector.
3. **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)** — Robot speed adapts to human proximity.
4. **Power and Force Limiting (PFL)** — Robot's mechanical output is biomechanically bounded.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the four modes.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Model Question, PERUT755]`
*Why is AR considered superior to conventional 2D HMIs for human-robot communication?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
AR delivers information **already pre-registered to the physical 3D workspace**, eliminating the cognitive effort of mentally mapping 2D data onto 3D scenes (reducing *transformational distance*). It enables:
- **Spatial intuition** — operators see robot intent where the action occurs.
- **Reduced cognitive load** — Fitts' Law-compliant pointing reduces task time.
- **Trust calibration** — visualized plans make robot behavior predictable.

*(1 mark per valid point, max 3.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students often **omit the units** when writing the separation-distance formula — KTU examiners deduct **1 mark** per missing unit.
> - Do **not confuse SSM with PFL** — SSM dynamically varies speed; PFL biomechanically limits force.
> - When asked to "explain AR visualization", students must give **at least one concrete example** (e.g., trajectory arrow, safety halo) — abstract statements alone lose **2–3 marks**.
> - Always cite the **standard** (ISO/TS 15066, ISO 10218-1) — failing to cite standards is a common reason for **partial marking**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, PERUT755]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)** collaborative mode. Derive the minimum separation distance formula and compute its value for $v_{robot} = 0.3$ m/s, $v_{human} = 1.5$ m/s, $T_r = t_r = 0.15$ s, $a_{max} = 1.2$ m/s², $S_{margin} = 0.05$ m. Discuss how AR visualizes this distance dynamically. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **three pillars** of AR-based human-robot communication. With a suitable architecture diagram, explain how an AR headset integrates robot state, perception, and safety data into a unified visualization pipeline. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *(7 marks: 2 for explanation + 3 for derivation + 1 for calculation + 1 for AR role)*

In **Speed and Separation Monitoring**, the robot's speed is continuously modulated so that it can always perform a protective stop before contacting a human. The governing equation:

$$
S_{min} = v_{human} T_{r} + v_{robot} t_{r} + \dfrac{v_{robot}^{2}}{2 a_{max}} + S_{margin}
$$

Numerical substitution:

$$
S_{min} = (1.5)(0.15) + (0.3)(0.15) + \dfrac{(0.3)^{2}}{2(1.2)} + 0.05
$$

$$
S_{min} = 0.225 + 0.045 + 0.0375 + 0.05 = 0.3575 \text{ m}
$$

**AR visualization role:** The computed $S_{min} = 0.36$ m is overlaid as a colored cylindrical zone around the robot envelope; the color transitions **green → yellow → red** as the human approaches, with a numeric HUD showing live distance and current safe-speed limit.

---

**(b)** *(7 marks: 3 for pillars + 2 for architecture explanation + 2 for integration discussion)*

The **three pillars** are:
1. **Intent Visualization** — robot's planned trajectory and goal.
2. **State & Perception Visualization** — robot's internal belief about the world.
3. **Safety & Trust Calibration** — legible safety boundaries.

The architecture pipeline (RGB + Depth + IMU → Visual-Inertial SLAM → 6DoF pose → calibration → AR overlay renderer → stereo display) feeds the human, whose voice/gesture feedback closes the loop to the robot controller. AR thus becomes the *bidirectional* communication medium — not just display but interaction surface.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **four collaborative operating modes** of a cobot as defined by ISO/TS 15066. For each mode, describe a corresponding AR visualization cue that a human operator would find intuitive. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **AR registration error budget**. Derive the total error formula assuming three independent error sources, and compute the result for $E_{track}=2$ mm, $E_{calib}=1$ mm, $E_{drift}=4$ mm. Comment on whether this error is acceptable for surgical cobot applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *(7 marks: 4 modes × 1 mark + 1 mark per AR cue linkage + 1 mark for diagram)*

| Mode | Description | AR Cue |
|---|---|---|
| **SRMS** | Stops when human enters | Red stop-halo |
| **Hand Guiding** | Operator moves arm manually | Blue trail showing recorded path |
| **SSM** | Speed adapts to distance | Dynamic color-ring + numeric HUD |
| **PFL** | Force/energy limited | Force-torque bar graph in AR |

---

**(b)** *(7 marks: 2 for definition + 3 for derivation + 1 for computation + 1 for surgical comment)*

$$
E_{total} = \sqrt{E_{track}^{2} + E_{calib}^{2} + E_{drift}^{2}} = \sqrt{4 + 1 + 16} = \sqrt{21} \approx 4.58 \text{ mm}
$$

For **surgical cobot applications**, the registration tolerance is typically **≤ 1 mm**. The computed 4.58 mm error is therefore **NOT acceptable** — additional fiducial markers, depth sensing, and multi-camera fusion are required to bring the error below the surgical threshold.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist**

- [ ] **Cobot definition** comes from **ISO/TS 15066** — *always cite the standard*.
- [ ] The **four collaborative modes** are SRMS, Hand Guiding, SSM, PFL — memorize in order.
- [ ] **SSM separation distance** formula has **four terms**: human encroachment + robot reaction + stopping distance + safety margin.
- [ ] **Stopping distance** scales as $v^{2}$ — quadratic in velocity — this is why cobots are speed-limited to ~250 mm/s.
- [ ] **AR registration error** follows **RSS (Root Sum of Squares)** model — not simple summation.
- [ ] **Fitts' Law** governs AR pointing task time: $MT = a + b \log_2(D/W + 1)$.
- [ ] The **three pillars** of AR-HRC: **Intent**, **State/Perception**, **Safety/Trust**.
- [ ] **Maximum AR latency** for comfortable perception: $\leq 20$ ms (one frame at 60 Hz minus jitter).
- [ ] **Color convention**: Green = safe, Yellow = caution, Red = stop.
- [ ] AR transforms HRC from **instructive** (text/screen) to **intuitive** (spatial).
- [ ] **Trust calibration** = Perceived reliability / Actual reliability — AR improves perceived reliability through transparency.
- [ ] In exam answers, **always show units** (m, mm, N, J) — KTU examiners penalize missing units.
- [ ] Surgical-grade AR requires **≤ 1 mm error** — typical consumer AR headsets give 3–5 mm — supplementary tracking is essential.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tracking Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT755 - VISUAL AND AUGMENTED REALITY FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tracking Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Tracking (in AR/VR/Robotics):** The continuous, real-time estimation of the **pose** (position + orientation) of one or more objects — typically a camera, a user, a robot end-effector, or a target — relative to a chosen **world reference frame**, with sufficient accuracy, precision, and low latency to enable stable registration of virtual content or closed-loop robotic control.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, a **Tracking System** is a sensor-and-algorithm pipeline that determines the **6 Degrees-of-Freedom (6DOF)** state of an object:

$$
\mathbf{x}(t) = \left[\, x,\, y,\, z,\, \phi,\, \theta,\, \psi \,\right]^{T}
$$

where $(x, y, z)$ is the 3D position and $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ are the roll-pitch-yaw Euler angles (or an equivalent rotation representation such as a quaternion).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Tracking is the **enabling sub-system** of every AR/VR/Robotic application. Without robust tracking, virtual objects "swim", drift, jitter, and break the illusion of being anchored to the real world.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of tracking like **Google Maps live navigation in your pocket**:

- Your phone has a **GPS sensor** (like an inertial sensor) and a **camera** (like a vision sensor).
- Maps software **fuses** these signals to continuously answer: *"Where am I, and which way am I facing?"*
- That fused answer (position + heading) is the **pose** — the same thing a tracking system produces for an AR headset or a robot.

Now scale that idea up:

| Real-World Analogy | AR/VR/Robotics Equivalent |
|---|---|
| GPS satellites | Outside-in optical trackers (e.g., Vicon, OptiTrack) |
| Phone IMU (gyro/accel) | Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) |
| Visual landmarks (buildings) | Fiducial markers / natural features |
| Sensor fusion in maps | Kalman / Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) |
| "You are here" arrow | 6DOF pose vector $\mathbf{x}(t)$ |

---

## 1.3 Why Tracking Matters in AR/VR/Robotics

Three canonical use-cases that make tracking indispensable:

1. **AR Registration** — A virtual surgical tumor must stay glued to the patient's anatomy even as the surgeon walks around. If the headset pose drifts, the tumor "swims" off-target → **patient safety risk**.
2. **VR Immersion** — A head-mounted display must update the rendered view within **$\leq 20$ ms** of head motion, otherwise users get **simulator sickness**.
3. **Robotic Manipulation** — A robot arm must know the 6DOF pose of a workpiece picked from a conveyor. Pose errors $\geq 2$ mm break insertion tasks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Performance Metrics (KTU 2024 expects recall of these):**
> - **Accuracy:** mean error in mm or degrees (systematic error).
> - **Precision / Repeatability:** standard deviation of repeated measurements (random error).
> - **Latency:** delay between motion and pose update, in **ms** (target $\leq 20$ ms for VR, $\leq 5$ ms for robotic control).
> - **Jitter:** high-frequency fluctuation in the pose signal.
> - **Robustness:** ability to maintain lock under occlusion, lighting change, or fast motion.
> - **Update Rate:** poses per second, in **Hz** (typical 60–1000 Hz).
> - **Workspace Volume:** the physical region inside which the tracker is reliable, in **m³**.

---

## 1.4 Coordinate Frames in Tracking

A tracking system juggles **multiple reference frames**:

| Frame | Symbol | Typical Origin |
|---|---|---|
| World | $\{W\}$ | Fixed lab / room origin |
| Tracker / Base | $\{B\}$ | Tracking station origin |
| Camera / Sensor | $\{C\}$ | Optical center of camera |
| Target / Object | $\{T\}$ | Geometric center of tracked body |
| Robot End-effector | $\{E\}$ | Tool center point (TCP) |

> The chain of homogeneous transforms $\,{}^{W}\mathbf{T}_{T} = {}^{W}\mathbf{T}_{B} \cdot {}^{B}\mathbf{T}_{C} \cdot {}^{C}\mathbf{T}_{T}\,$ is the central kinematic equation KTU examiners love to test.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Tracking Systems

Tracking systems fall into **three master families**, distinguished by their **primary sensing modality**:

### A. Sensor-Based (Non-Visual) Trackers

1. **Magnetic Trackers** — measure the field of AC-driven coils. Pros: small, no line-of-sight. Cons: severely disturbed by metal/electronics.
2. **Inertial Trackers (IMU)** — gyroscope + accelerometer (± magnetometer) integrated to give orientation and translational drift. Pros: very high update rate ($\geq 1$ kHz), no external infrastructure. Cons: integrates noise → drift.
3. **Acoustic / Ultrasonic Trackers** — measure time-of-flight of ultrasound between emitters and microphones.
4. **Mechanical / Linkage Trackers** — boom arm with joint encoders (e.g., Microscribe).
5. **RF / UWB Trackers** — measure time-of-flight or angle-of-arrival of radio signals.
6. **Hybrid (e.g., Inertial + Magnetic = InterSense IS-900)** — sensor fusion blends two or more modalities.

### B. Vision-Based (Optical) Trackers

1. **Outside-In, Marker-Based** — fixed cameras observe active or passive markers on the target (e.g., **Vicon, OptiTrack, ARToolKit fiducials**).
2. **Inside-Out, Marker-Based** — head-mounted camera observes fiducials in the environment (e.g., ARToolKit, AprilTags, ARUco).
3. **Inside-Out, Markerless / Natural-Feature** — camera observes texture/keypoints in the environment (e.g., **SLAM, Visual-Inertial Odometry, ARKit, ARCore**).
4. **Model-Based (3D Object Tracking)** — explicit 3D CAD model of the target is rendered and matched to image edges (e.g., **EDGE tracking** by Drummond & Cipolla).

### C. Hybrid Sensor Fusion

Almost every commercial system today is a **hybrid**: an **IMU** for high-rate short-term motion + a **vision module** for long-term drift correction, joined by an **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** or **Error-State Kalman Filter (ESKF)**.

---

## 2.2 Inside-Out vs Outside-In (Frequently Tested)

| Property | **Inside-Out** | **Outside-In** |
|---|---|---|
| Sensors on | The moving object / headset | The environment |
| Reference frame | World | Tracker base |
| Workspace | Unlimited (in principle) | Limited by tracker coverage |
| Typical accuracy | mm–cm | sub-mm |
| Drift | Yes, must be corrected | Minimal |
| Examples | HoloLens, Meta Quest, ARKit | Vicon, OptiTrack, ART |

---

## 2.3 Pose Representation

A 6DOF rigid transform can be expressed in three interchangeable forms:

### (i) 3×3 Rotation Matrix $\mathbf{R}$
$$
\mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix} r_{11} & r_{12} & r_{13} \\ r_{21} & r_{22} & r_{23} \\ r_{31} & r_{32} & r_{33} \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{R}^{T}\mathbf{R}=\mathbf{I},\ \det(\mathbf{R})=+1
$$

### (ii) Euler Angles $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ — roll, pitch, yaw
ZYX (yaw–pitch–roll) convention:
$$
\mathbf{R}_{ZYX}(\phi,\theta,\psi)=\mathbf{R}_{z}(\psi)\,\mathbf{R}_{y}(\theta)\,\mathbf{R}_{x}(\phi)
$$
$$
\mathbf{R}_{z}(\psi)=\begin{bmatrix}\cos\psi & -\sin\psi & 0\\ \sin\psi & \cos\psi & 0\\ 0 & 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}
$$
$$
\mathbf{R}_{y}(\theta)=\begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & 0 & \sin\theta\\ 0 & 1 & 0\\ -\sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta\end{bmatrix}
$$
$$
\mathbf{R}_{x}(\phi)=\begin{bmatrix}1 & 0 & 0\\ 0 & \cos\phi & -\sin\phi\\ 0 & \sin\phi & \cos\phi\end{bmatrix}
$$

### (iii) Unit Quaternion $\mathbf{q} = [q_w, q_x, q_y, q_z]^T$
$$
\mathbf{q} = \begin{bmatrix} q_w \\ \mathbf{q}_{v}\end{bmatrix},\quad \vert\vert \mathbf{q} \vert\vert = 1
$$
Rotating a vector $\mathbf{v}$:
$$
\mathbf{v}' = \mathbf{q} \otimes \begin{bmatrix}0\\ \mathbf{v}\end{bmatrix} \otimes \mathbf{q}^{-1}
$$
> [!NOTE]
> **Why quaternions?** No gimbal lock, continuous interpolation via **SLERP**, and only 4 parameters vs 9 in a matrix.

---

## 2.4 The Homogeneous Transform

$$
{}^{A}\mathbf{T}_{B} = \begin{bmatrix} {}^{A}\mathbf{R}_{B} & {}^{A}\mathbf{t}_{B} \\ \mathbf{0}^{T} & 1 \end{bmatrix} \in SE(3)
$$

A 3D point $\mathbf{p}_{B}$ in frame $\{B\}$ is mapped to $\{A\}$ by:
$$
\tilde{\mathbf{p}}_{A} = {}^{A}\mathbf{T}_{B}\,\tilde{\mathbf{p}}_{B}
$$

where $\tilde{\mathbf{p}}$ denotes homogeneous coordinates $(\mathbf{p},1)^{T}$.

---

## 2.5 Vision-Based Tracking: The Pinhole Camera

A camera with focal length $f$ projects a 3D point $\mathbf{P}=(X,Y,Z)^{T}$ to image pixel $\mathbf{p}=(u,v)^{T}$:
$$
\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \sim \mathbf{K}\,{}^{C}\mathbf{T}_{W}\begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$
with the **intrinsic matrix**:
$$
\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & s & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$
where $f_x = f/s_x$, $f_y=f/s_y$ are focal lengths in pixels, $(c_x,c_y)$ the principal point, and $s$ the skew.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table. It is the single most testable part of Module 3.

| # | Concept | Formula / Statement | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 6DOF pose | $\mathbf{x}=[x,y,z,\phi,\theta,\psi]^{T}$ | m, rad |
| 2 | Homogeneous transform | ${}^{A}\mathbf{T}_{B}\in SE(3)$ | — |
| 3 | Rotation orthogonality | $\mathbf{R}^{T}\mathbf{R}=\mathbf{I}$ | — |
| 4 | Chain of transforms | ${}^{W}\mathbf{T}_{T} = {}^{W}\mathbf{T}_{B}\cdot{}^{B}\mathbf{T}_{C}\cdot{}^{C}\mathbf{T}_{T}$ | — |
| 5 | Pinhole projection | $u=f_x X/Z + c_x,\ v=f_y Y/Z + c_y$ | pixel |
| 6 | Focal length in pixels | $f_x = f / s_x$ | pixel |
| 7 | Depth | $Z = f B / d$ (stereo) | m |
| 8 | Quaternion norm | $\vert\vert \mathbf{q} \vert\vert = 1$ | — |
| 9 | SLERP | $\mathbf{q}(t)=\frac{\sin((1-t)\Omega)}{\sin\Omega}\mathbf{q}_0+\frac{\sin(t\Omega)}{\sin\Omega}\mathbf{q}_1$ | — |
| 10 | KF Predict | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1}=\mathbf{F}_k\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1\vert k-1}$ | — |
| 11 | KF Covariance Predict | $\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1}=\mathbf{F}_k\mathbf{P}_{k-1\vert k-1}\mathbf{F}_k^{T}+\mathbf{Q}_k$ | — |
| 12 | KF Innovation | $\mathbf{y}_k = \mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{H}_k\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1}$ | — |
| 13 | Kalman gain | $\mathbf{K}_k = \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1}\mathbf{H}_k^{T}\mathbf{S}_k^{-1}$ | — |
| 14 | KF Update | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k}=\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1}+\mathbf{K}_k\mathbf{y}_k$ | — |
| 15 | Update covariance | $\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k} = (\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{K}_k\mathbf{H}_k)\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1}$ | — |
| 16 | RMSE | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\vert\vert \mathbf{e}_i \vert\vert^{2}}$ | m |
| 17 | Angular error | $\theta_{err} = 2\arccos(\vert q_w \vert)$ | rad |
| 18 | Resolution limit (optical) | $\Delta\theta = 1.22 \lambda / D$ (rad) | rad |
| 19 | Optical latency budget | $t_{end\text{-}to\text{-}end} \leq 20$ ms (VR) | ms |
| 20 | Drift rate (gyro) | $\text{drift} = b_g$ rad/s bias | rad/s |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Surgical AR (e.g., HoloLens in OR):** inside-out SLAM with sub-mm equivalent accuracy.
- **Industrial robot calibration:** outside-in optical trackers (Leica, FARO) reach **±0.05 mm** over 10 m.
- **Drone navigation:** visual-inertial odometry (VIO) using EKF fusion of camera + IMU.
- **VR gaming:** SLAM inside-out tracking (Quest 3) at 72–120 Hz, 6DOF.
- **Autonomous vehicles:** multi-sensor fusion of LiDAR + camera + GPS/INS using EKF/UKF.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section performs the **four derivations** that KTU examiners set most often in Part B.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Homogeneous Transform Chain for a Robot–Camera System

> **Problem statement.** A robot base $\{B\}$ stands at the world origin. A camera $\{C\}$ is mounted on the end-effector $\{E\}$. The base→end-effector transform is ${}^{B}\mathbf{T}_{E}$. The camera is offset from the TCP by ${}^{E}\mathbf{T}_{C}$. A marker frame $\{M\}$ is observed in the camera. Derive ${}^{W}\mathbf{T}_{M}$.

### Step 1 — Identify the chain of frames

World $\to$ Base $\to$ End-effector $\to$ Camera $\to$ Marker: $\{W\}\to\{B\}\to\{E\}\to\{C\}\to\{M\}$.

### Step 2 — Write each link

$$
{}^{W}\mathbf{T}_{B} = \begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{I}_{3} & \mathbf{0}\\ \mathbf{0}^{T} & 1\end{bmatrix}
\quad (\text{base at world origin})
$$

$$
{}^{B}\mathbf{T}_{E} = \begin{bmatrix}{}^{B}\mathbf{R}_{E} & {}^{B}\mathbf{t}_{E}\\ \mathbf{0}^{T} & 1\end{bmatrix}
\quad (\text{forward kinematics from joint angles})
$$

$$
{}^{E}\mathbf{T}_{C} = \begin{bmatrix}{}^{E}\mathbf{R}_{C} & {}^{E}\mathbf{t}_{C}\\ \mathbf{0}^{T} & 1\end{bmatrix}
\quad (\text{hand-eye calibration result})
$$

$$
{}^{C}\mathbf{T}_{M} = \begin{bmatrix}{}^{C}\mathbf{R}_{M} & {}^{C}\mathbf{t}_{M}\\ \mathbf{0}^{T} & 1\end{bmatrix}
\quad (\text{marker pose from PnP solver})
$$

### Step 3 — Compose right-to-left

$$
{}^{W}\mathbf{T}_{M} = {}^{W}\mathbf{T}_{B}\cdot{}^{B}\mathbf{T}_{E}\cdot{}^{E}\mathbf{T}_{C}\cdot{}^{C}\mathbf{T}_{M}
$$

### Step 4 — Substituting and simplifying

$$
{}^{W}\mathbf{T}_{M} = \mathbf{I}\cdot{}^{B}\mathbf{T}_{E}\cdot{}^{E}\mathbf{T}_{C}\cdot{}^{C}\mathbf{T}_{M}
$$

$$
\boxed{\ {}^{W}\mathbf{T}_{M} = {}^{B}\mathbf{T}_{E}\cdot{}^{E}\mathbf{T}_{C}\cdot{}^{C}\mathbf{T}_{M}\ }
$$

### Step 5 — Extract pose components

$$
{}^{W}\mathbf{R}_{M} = {}^{B}\mathbf{R}_{E}\cdot{}^{E}\mathbf{R}_{C}\cdot{}^{C}\mathbf{R}_{M}
$$
$$
{}^{W}\mathbf{t}_{M} = {}^{B}\mathbf{R}_{E}\cdot{}^{E}\mathbf{R}_{C}\cdot{}^{C}\mathbf{t}_{M} + {}^{B}\mathbf{R}_{E}\cdot{}^{E}\mathbf{t}_{C} + {}^{B}\mathbf{t}_{E}
$$

> This is the **operational backbone** of every AR-robotics application: compute each link, multiply, and read off the marker pose in world coordinates.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pinhole Projection of a 3D Point

> **Problem.** Given $\mathbf{P}_{W}=(X,Y,Z)^{T}$ in world and camera extrinsic ${}^{C}\mathbf{T}_{W}$, derive the pixel coordinates $(u,v)$.

### Step 1 — Transform world point to camera frame

$$
\begin{bmatrix}X_{C}\\Y_{C}\\Z_{C}\\1\end{bmatrix} = {}^{C}\mathbf{T}_{W}\begin{bmatrix}X\\Y\\Z\\1\end{bmatrix}
$$

Expanding:
$$
X_{C} = r_{11}X + r_{12}Y + r_{13}Z + t_x
$$
$$
Y_{C} = r_{21}X + r_{22}Y + r_{23}Z + t_y
$$
$$
Z_{C} = r_{31}X + r_{32}Y + r_{33}Z + t_z
$$

### Step 2 — Apply perspective division

The image-plane (metric) coordinates are:
$$
x = X_{C}/Z_{C},\quad y = Y_{C}/Z_{C}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply intrinsics

$$
\begin{bmatrix}u\\v\\1\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & s & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}X_{C}/Z_{C}\\ Y_{C}/Z_{C}\\ 1\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4 — Final result

$$
\boxed{\ u = f_x\frac{X_{C}}{Z_{C}} + s\frac{Y_{C}}{Z_{C}} + c_x,\qquad v = f_y\frac{Y_{C}}{Z_{C}} + c_y\ }
$$

> For modern cameras skew $s \approx 0$, $c_x \approx W/2$, $c_y \approx H/2$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — 2D-to-3D Pose from a Square Fiducial (PnP)

> **Problem.** A square marker of side $2a$ has its four corners $\mathbf{P}_i$ in the marker frame at $(\pm a,\pm a,0)^{T}$. Their image pixel coordinates $\mathbf{p}_i=(u_i,v_i)^{T}$ are detected. Derive the camera pose ${}^{C}\mathbf{T}_{M}$.

### Step 1 — Build the 3D model

$$
\mathbf{P}_1 = (a,a,0)^{T},\ \mathbf{P}_2 = (-a,a,0)^{T},\ \mathbf{P}_3 = (-a,-a,0)^{T},\ \mathbf{P}_4 = (a,-a,0)^{T}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute image homography

Solve $\mathbf{p}_i \sim \mathbf{H}\tilde{\mathbf{P}}_i$ via DLT (Direct Linear Transform) using the 4 correspondences (8 equations, 8 unknowns in $\mathbf{H}$).

### Step 3 — Extract rotation and translation from $\mathbf{H}$

Let $\mathbf{H} = [\mathbf{h}_1 \ \mathbf{h}_2 \ \mathbf{h}_3]$. With $\mathbf{K}$ known from calibration:
$$
{}^{C}\mathbf{R}_{M} = \begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{r}_1 & \mathbf{r}_2 & \mathbf{r}_1 \times \mathbf{r}_2\end{bmatrix}
$$
where
$$
\mathbf{r}_1 = \lambda\mathbf{K}^{-1}\mathbf{h}_1,\qquad \mathbf{r}_2 = \lambda\mathbf{K}^{-1}\mathbf{h}_2
$$
with
$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{\vert\vert \mathbf{K}^{-1}\mathbf{h}_1 \vert\vert} = \frac{1}{\vert\vert \mathbf{K}^{-1}\mathbf{h}_2 \vert\vert}
$$
$$
{}^{C}\mathbf{t}_{M} = \lambda\mathbf{K}^{-1}\mathbf{h}_3
$$

### Step 4 — Orthogonalize $\mathbf{R}$ via SVD

Because $\mathbf{r}_1,\mathbf{r}_2$ are noisy, enforce $\mathbf{R}^{T}\mathbf{R}=\mathbf{I}$ by SVD:
$$
\mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{T} = \mathbf{R}\quad\Rightarrow\quad \mathbf{R}^{*} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{V}^{T}
$$
This is the **orthogonal Procrustes** step.

### Step 5 — Final pose

$$
\boxed{\ {}^{C}\mathbf{T}_{M} = \begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{R}^{*} & \mathbf{t}_{M}\\ \mathbf{0}^{T} & 1\end{bmatrix}\ }
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Discrete Kalman Filter for 1D Constant-Velocity Position Tracking

> **Problem.** An object moves with (nearly) constant velocity $v$ along $x$. The position $x_k$ is measured by a noisy sensor with variance $R$. Process noise variance is $Q$. Derive the optimal recursive estimator.

### Step 1 — Define the state

$$
\mathbf{x}_k = \begin{bmatrix}x_k\\ \dot{x}_k\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}\text{position}\\ \text{velocity}\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2 — Write the constant-velocity state equation

$$
\mathbf{x}_k = \mathbf{F}\mathbf{x}_{k-1} + \mathbf{w}_k,\quad \mathbf{w}_k \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0},\mathbf{Q})
$$
$$
\mathbf{F} = \begin{bmatrix}1 & \Delta t\\ 0 & 1\end{bmatrix},\quad \mathbf{Q} = \begin{bmatrix}\sigma_x^{2} & 0\\ 0 & \sigma_v^{2}\end{bmatrix}
$$

Position update: $x_k = x_{k-1} + \dot{x}_{k-1}\Delta t$.
Velocity update: $\dot{x}_k = \dot{x}_{k-1} + \text{noise}$.

### Step 3 — Measurement equation

$$
z_k = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{x}_k + n_k,\quad n_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0,R)
$$
$$
\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 0\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4 — Predict step

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1} = \mathbf{F}\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1\vert k-1}
$$
$$
\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} = \mathbf{F}\mathbf{P}_{k-1\vert k-1}\mathbf{F}^{T} + \mathbf{Q}
$$

### Step 5 — Innovation and Kalman gain

$$
y_k = z_k - \mathbf{H}\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1}
$$
$$
\mathbf{S}_k = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1}\mathbf{H}^{T} + R
$$
$$
\mathbf{K}_k = \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1}\mathbf{H}^{T}\mathbf{S}_k^{-1}
$$

### Step 6 — Update step

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1} + \mathbf{K}_k y_k
$$
$$
\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{H})\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1}
$$

### Step 7 — Resulting 2×2 gain

For this specific 1D-velocity system:
$$
\boxed{\ \mathbf{K}_k = \begin{bmatrix}k_{1}\\ k_{2}\end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{S_k}\begin{bmatrix}P_{11} + \Delta t\, P_{12}\\ P_{12} + \Delta t\, P_{22}\end{bmatrix}\ }
$$
with $S_k = P_{11} + R$. The estimator optimally blends model prediction and sensor measurement, achieving the minimum-mean-square-error (MMSE) position estimate.

> [!NOTE]
> For nonlinear tracking (e.g., camera pose), the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** linearises the measurement model via Jacobians. Modern systems use the **Error-State Kalman Filter (ESKF)** or **Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF)** instead.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Tracker Trade-offs

| Tracker | Accuracy | Range | Latency | Drift | Occlusion Robust | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Magnetic** (Polhemus) | 1–3 mm | 1–3 m | 4 ms | Low | High | Medium |
| **Inertial (IMU)** | n/a (drifts) | n/a | < 1 ms | High | High | Low |
| **Optical Outside-In** (Vicon) | 0.1–1 mm | 10 m | 3–10 ms | Low | Medium | High |
| **Optical Inside-Out (Marker)** | 1–5 mm | Scene | 10 ms | Low | Low | Low |
| **Optical Inside-Out (SLAM)** | 5–50 mm | Scene | 15–30 ms | Medium | Medium | Low |
| **Hybrid VIO** | 1–10 mm | Scene | 5–15 ms | Low | High | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Tracking System Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENS["SENSOR LAYER"]
        cam["Camera RGB"]
        imu["IMU 9-axis"]
        mag["Magnetic Coil"]
        gps["GPS / UWB"]
    end

    subgraph PRE["PRE-PROCESSING"]
        cal["Calibration"]
        det["Feature / Marker Detection"]
        sync["Time Synchronization"]
    end

    subgraph EST["STATE ESTIMATION"]
        pnp["PnP / ICP"]
        kf["Kalman / EKF"]
        slam["Visual SLAM Frontend"]
        ba["Bundle Adjustment Backend"]
    end

    subgraph OUT["OUTPUT"]
        pose["6DOF Pose"]
        cov["Covariance"]
        qual["Quality Flag"]
    end

    APP["AR Renderer / Robot Controller"]

    cam --> det
    imu --> sync
    mag --> sync
    gps --> sync
    det --> pnp
    det --> slam
    cal --> pnp
    cal --> slam
    pnp --> kf
    imu --> kf
    slam --> ba
    ba --> kf
    kf --> pose
    kf --> cov
    kf --> qual
    pose --> APP
    cov --> APP
    qual --> APP
```

> This block diagram is **the single most important visual** for KTU 2024 — examiners frequently ask students to label the blocks in this pipeline.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Tracking Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T["Tracking Systems"]
    T --> SB["Sensor-Based"]
    T --> VB["Vision-Based"]
    T --> HY["Hybrid Fusion"]

    SB --> SB1["Magnetic"]
    SB --> SB2["Inertial IMU"]
    SB --> SB3["Acoustic"]
    SB --> SB4["RF / UWB"]
    SB --> SB5["Mechanical"]

    VB --> VB1["Outside-In Marker"]
    VB --> VB2["Inside-Out Marker"]
    VB --> VB3["Inside-Out Markerless SLAM"]
    VB --> VB4["Model-Based 3D"]

    HY --> HY1["Visual-Inertial VIO"]
    HY --> HY2["INS + GPS"]
    HY --> HY3["LiDAR + Camera + IMU"]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Coordinate-Frame Chain (Eye-in-Hand Robot)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W["World {W}"] --> B["Robot Base {B}"]
    B --> E["End-Effector {E}"]
    E --> C["Camera {C}"]
    C --> M["Marker / Object {M}"]
```

> Each arrow represents one homogeneous transform ${}^{parent}\mathbf{T}_{child}$.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Kalman Filter Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["State Estimate x̂(k-1)"] --> P["Predict: x̂(k|k-1) = F x̂(k-1)"]
    PP["Covariance P(k-1)"] --> PCP["Predict Cov: P(k|k-1) = F P F^T + Q"]
    P --> INN["Innovation: y = z - H x̂(k|k-1)"]
    Z["Sensor z(k)"] --> INN
    PCP --> KG["Kalman Gain: K = P H^T S^-1"]
    INN --> UP["Update: x̂(k) = x̂(k|k-1) + K y"]
    KG --> UP
    UP --> OUT["Pose Output"]
    UP --> P2["New Estimate x̂(k)"]
    UP --> PP2["New Covariance P(k)"]
    P2 --> P
    PP2 --> PCP
```

---

## 4.5 Notes on Mermaid Safeguards

> [!NOTE]
> All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `cam`, `kf`, `slam`) and prefixed with letters to avoid clashes with mermaid reserved words (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`). Labels contain raw uppercase / lowercase text only, with no bold/italic markers or HTML tables inside the quoted labels.
